title: Why are marriage rates falling? | Institute for Family Studies
gist: IFS highlights a Washington Post 'Make It Make Sense' podcast episode where sociologist Brad Wilcox frames a 'marriage paradox' -- young adults rejecting marriage despite data linking it to greater happiness and financial success -- and pushes back on what he calls the media's anti-family narrative.

insights.claim: Young adults are increasingly rejecting marriage even though data shows marriage correlates with greater happiness and financial success -- a dynamic the episode frames as the 'marriage paradox.'
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: A persistent gap between documented marriage benefits and declining uptake signals durable demographic/consumer-formation headwinds rather than a temporary blip.
insights.quote: Why are young adults increasingly rejecting marriage despite clear data showing it leads to greater happiness and financial success?
insights.locator: Why are young adults increasingly rejecting marriage

insights.claim: The episode argues married mothers and fathers report significantly higher levels of meaning, stability, and prosperity than mainstream media narratives about marriage acknowledge.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Positions marriage as an under-messaged driver of household stability and prosperity -- relevant to any thesis on family-formation-linked spending and savings behavior.
insights.quote: debunk the mainstream media's anti-family narrative, explaining why married mothers and fathers report significantly higher levels of meaning, stability and prosperity

insights.claim: The conversation treats the 'tradwife' movement as a notable, controversial response within the modern dating and gender-role landscape that young men and women are navigating.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: A cultural counter-trend worth tracking as a leading indicator of shifting gender-role attitudes among young adults.

insights.claim: The episode identifies casual hookup culture as having real-world consequences that are entangled with the broader decline in marriage rates.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Suggests dating-market structure, not just economics, is a contributing driver of the marriage decline worth separating out in any demographic model.
insights.locator: the real-world consequences of casual hookup culture
